canvass_story: I primarily canvassed in Kensington - a very polarized neighborhood with a large (pro-Zohran) Bangledeshi population, as well as a newer young professional/hipster/DSA type population (pro-Zohran) and a number of Hasidic Jews (very anti-Zohran). I was harassed personally and had canvassers harassed by Hasidic Jews. But one time we were launching a canvas and a 17 year old Hasidic kid showed up wanting to canvas - I sent him out with two exerienced canvassers (one wearing a Keffiyah) and he came back and had had the best time. We tried to give him merch but he said he'd only have to hide it at home. But he came back multiple times, and it was incredibly moving.
was_asked_more_desc: I went to a canvassing training right before the general campaign launch and volunteered to do a role play. i was then asked by office volunteers to train as a field lead which I did.
asked_others_desc: I encouraged a fellow office volunteer to come out and canvas and promises to canvass with them if they came to a shift I was leading. She begrudgingly came, had fun and then did all day poll site visibility on election day.
post_collab: Volunteered together on another campaign
final_thoughts: This was the best run campaign I've ever been a part of. The community it built was warm and supportive, and you felt it throughout the city. There was a sense of community and a love for NY that really was palpable everywhere I went. And even though I am not very merch driven as a field lead - the merch was KEY. There was a whole merch economy.

canvass_story: On the Monday before election day, I was paired with a new canvasser (young Muslim woman) on the Upper East side. Since it was ~3p or so, not many people were home. We went into a building across from the park where we launched and our first few conversations went well. However, after knocking on someone's door, we were asked how we got into the building, and when we said a delivery worker let us in, we were told to sit in the hallways and we were under "citizen's arrest" while he called the cops. I immediately contacted the field lead, and many informed us that citizen's arrest wasn't real in NYC, but this was a large man, and I felt unsafe leaving, especially with a young woman of color with me. Our field leads handled the situation wonderfully, also contacting law enforcement and reminded us that we had done nothing wrong. Law enforcement reminded the man of this when they "freed" us, and many kind volunteers came to support us after, walking us back to the park (and offering to take us home, though we both declined). It was scary to be in this position of power, but something about defending my fellow canvasser got me through it calmly and strongly. I was shocked at the behavior of my neighbor, mostly because we would've left the building if given the opportunity. I will never forget this moment, but on election day, I continued doing pollsite visibility, because I wouldn't let one angry man stop me.

final_thoughts: This campaign set me up for success in NYC. Even despite my crazy experiences, I felt I had something to connect with others over, and I know it inspired my family in their own communities.

canvass_story: When canvassing in Harlem, I came across a doorman co-op that would not allow me entry despite my pleading. I then loaded MiniVan and came across a name in my voter list who had signed the petition for Zohran to appear on the ballot then cold-called him. He said he could not allow me into the building, but he did propose a separate offer of convening a town hall amongst his and neighboring co-op buildings to answer questions and pitch Zohran. We set a date and I reached out to the campaign for help, who connected me with someone more knowledgeable on policy. On the evening of the town hall, I was expecting a small crowd and a ~30 min Q&A but instead came upon a packed room of primarily educators comprising the HFDC co-op community who had such a fantastic array of questions that we all spoke for nearly ~90 min instead. While I could provide all assurances that Zohran would address their every need or concern, I do feel I successfully convinced many to give him and his new anti-corporate, grassroots approach to politics a chance by putting him on their rank lists.

canvass_story: I remember canvassing NYCHA housing buildings a few times in the Lower East Side, and a majority of people I spoke to were Hispanic immigrants. It was very common in these buildings for people to ask interrogatively who I was before they felt comfortable opening their doors, and to then be the nicest people in conversation. It took me a little while to realize it, but the early suspicion they held of me was because they were afraid I was ICE. That stuck with me, and it was very informative about just how terrifying it is to be an immigrant right now.

The second season of Andor came out in April 2025, and it completely changed my life. I was reminded of Nemik's manifesto in the show, about the need to keep trying in the face of oppression until we've finally won. I started having this overwhelming feeling that I needed to do whatever I could to make sure Mamdani won, because I realized I wouldn't be able to live with myself if my inaction led to Cuomo winning. After a couple days of meditating on Andor's message, I found Mamdani's campaign website and signed up to volunteer

canvass_story:
Near the end of the campaign, as early voting was starting to get tallied and we started seeing the exit polls, I think many of us started feeling a bit concerned that Cuomo might edge out Mamdani. In the final week, many others and I canvassed every single day leading up to election day, and what is most salient about that experience is how motivated everyone was to make sure Mamdani won. During the final weekend before election day, I canvassed and led teams from sunrise to sundown both days. It was exhausting, and it was starting to get colder outside, but like many others who kept coming back to canvass more, I knew I wouldn't be able to live with myself if I didn't do everything I could to make sure our guy won. 

On election night, I and hundreds of other canvassers went to Bohemian Hall & Beer Garden in Astoria to watch the news, and when it became clear that Mamdani was going to win, the celebration we had that night will live with me forever. It was a moment of true catharsis.
was_asked_more_desc: Before I became a field lead, I had just signed up to canvass. After the first day of canvassing, I mentioned to my field lead that I wanted to do more and be more involved. He sent me information about field lead training, which I immediately signed up for
post_collab: Volunteered together on another campaign
Attended protests or demonstrations together
final_thoughts: Volunteering changed my life. It has completely reoriented my own value system as well as my place in broader society. It taught me the value of community based on common goals rather than on heritage or tradition. Since the campaign, I have become much more involved in local community efforts, such as food pantry distribution and other mutual aid services. Mamdani's campaign showed me what was possible when we live in a city unified by hope rather than divided by fear

I then scoured to the Volunteer page and saw that there was a volunteer option very close to me, literally a 10 min bus ride, so I signed up.

canvass_story: Like I mentioned earlier, I was already good at talking to strangers on the street about something I cared about. I’ve also always been publicly politically opinionated, discussing politics with friends, family, even colleagues. Zohran Mamdani’s mayoral campaign became a natural outlet for me to place my energy to. In the beginning, I felt anxious and nervous, but after a few tries, it became natural to me. After a canvassing shift, I felt energized, and that’s how I learned that I’m actually an extrovert. I learned that I’m actually really good at talking to other volunteers and managing other’s feelings and expectations. I think I honed in on a leadership role and I’m definitely a braver person today than before the campaign.

final_thoughts: I often say that Zohran’s campaign changed my life, its made me a better communicator, open to have hard conversations with people I may disagree with, I feel more connected with my neighbors and city at large, I’m more brave and confident in my abilities. I am now volunteering my time with a congressional campaign for NY13, where I’m assuming a bit more leadership responsibilities along other volunteers I met during the Zohran campaign. Organizing has been a great way to connect with other politically minded people and it feels like a big ongoing project for a better world.

2: People were so inspired that there were those who didn't live in nyc full time, or maybe moved out because they were outpriced, flying and commuting just to volunteer to help out.
3: I got canvassed on one of my off days.
was_asked_more_desc: I got asked to be a field lead. And for one time I got asked to up my responsibility and coordinate not only the canvassers but a celebrity meet and greet with the volunteers.
asked_others_desc: I asked my friends to come volunteer, and even asked them to try to become field leads with me. They turned me down, but did support the campaign by sharing posts and voting.
post_collab: Other (please specify)
final_thoughts: I cannot express how much hope was involved in this campaign. At a certain point it wasn't even about Mamdani, but what he represented. After everything thats happened since Trump was elected, it felt like having my head held under water then suddenly having someone take the weight off my head, and I could sit up and breathe finally. I know that the work is far from over, I know the dangers of making one man a demigogue, I'm sure Mayor Mamdani would agree. But it's about so much more than one person. Honestly, it's about so much more than one city. It's about all of us. It's about the future. It's hope. And isn't that what it means to be an American? To dream and hope for a better tomorrow, and then build it.

final_thoughts:
I think it's important to remember that the campaign was built on a blueprint forged by DSA in NYC and elsewhere. What was new was that it was a winnable campaign for a big seat with national attention, though the Zohran campaign specifically did create a lot of that "winnability" through strategy and their actual platform. 

While I differ philosophically from DSA, and even from electoralism somewhat, one must place credit where credit is due. This was a clear chance to radically improve local government by electing someone who was not beholden to wealthy and corporate donors. That was enough for me to get involved, and enough for a lot of people to vote. And the campaign succeeded on the back of lots of volunteer labor that most people simply will not do for a mainstream corporate-aligned Democrat, quite rightly. 

And frankly, given the DSA canvassing blueprint and some pretty straightforward policy ideas, voting for Zohran was often an easy sell. People absolutely want to see politicians diverge from the mainstream Democratic party and its corporate partners, and offer clear policies that benefit regular people. It is absolutely just that simple.

canvass_story:
During the last days before the primary election, I was knocking doors during an evening shift on a hot night in Sunset Park. I was standing on a doorstep hoping somebody would answer and looking down at the street below me. I saw two neighbors, one Hispanic and one Chinese, wave to each other. They started speaking to each other haltingly, in each other's languages. The former yelled out "ni hao" to his neighbor, who promptly replied that it was "muy caliente" that evening. 

This stood out to me because the campaign cycle had been dominated by fearmongering and hateful rhetoric from our opponents, and to see this simple exchange heartened me and made me remember what we were fighting for--namely, the right of the working class of this polyglot city to live and coexist in comfort and friendship, no matter where in the world they were originally from.

canvass_story: I have been canvassing since I was around 15, and I am 21 now, but I remember field leading in the Upper West Side and training one of my friends who is Jewish and lives up there. She got very antagonistic with another Jewish voter who hated Mamdani, and would be very open to Jewish people about her pro-Mamdani stance and would be extremely anti-Cuomo. It was difficult to tell her that was not our job -- we can't convince everyone, and I found it very hard to both dissuade random people from harassing me and following me around for being pro-Mamdani on my shifts, and also tell volunteers that they can walk away from conversations as well.
was_asked_more_desc: I don't know if this is "greater responsibility", but I had a shift in Morningside Heights with another field lead that would ask me over and over to go outside and check in on volunteers and give them more material throughout the entire day. This was a senior field lead, and I was much younger and it was one of my first field lead shifts in Morningside Heights. I was on shift this day from 9am to 8pm, and my feet were hurting horribly and I told her I needed to rest, but she said I needed to go out and help people since I had to give them more material or we needed more assistance in a certain area. She was inside the whole day and was doing "managerial" work, talking to volunteers, while I had to go out and do on the ground work. I was limping by the time we had to train the 5pm shift since I had been walking to volunteers at far away locations all day, and she still asked me to carry things back for her to the apartment we were working out of. I later told the campaign leads about this incident and they said she would not be matched with younger field leads, since she had a history of doing this to others.

He was a long time community organizer and I missed his intelligence, kindness, and adeptness at engaging large groups of volunteers/people he didn't know. I also felt stuck working in hospitality and that I would need a graduate degree to do anything else but couldn't afford one. Shortly after we broke up, my ex posted on social media about his involvement in the field operation often and I signed up, I believe, sometime in February - partially out of jealousy, partially out of grief, partially because Zohran's media presence seemed to have real traction - there was something solid about it, partically because people I respected in my social circle or people I aspired to be like were also getting involved, and partially because it was free + developmentally meaningful.

canvass_story: I trend skepitcal so I have a hard time giving motivational speeches. I prefer to focus on logistics and outcome, but I remember leading one of my last "regular" canvasses before GOTV for the primary - It was a big group, some of the volunteers I had seen weekly, a lot were new and I had been leading canvasses for a couple months at that point. I decided to try a motivational speech and I said something true and I tried to say it with some sort of gusto and sincerity. I believe I said, "before you guys go out, I'm going to tell you something another lead said to me which at the time, I fully did not believe, but now I have come to believe and it is that, doorknocking wins elections. What you are about to go do in the next three hours is help win this election." I couldn't really look at anyone while I was saying it but after I did, I looked up and saw probably 10 pairs of eyes that had become a lot brighter than they were 30 seconds prior (I think!) and I knew that I was doing the thing I was supposed to do as a lead which was motivate people, and I was doing it not because I like to (if its not clear I really hate doing that sort of thing) and it worked and I was able to give people something that was given to me which was, I think, a real sense of purpose and a real sense of agency; not for the rest of their lives, not even for the next three hours probably, but just in that moment and that was a really really good feeling. Probably the best feeling.
was_asked_more_desc: yes, I became close friends with my field coordinator (still to this day!) She would ask me to do 1:1 trainings or provide support to other volunteers, or I would get asked by different field coordinators to help run large campaign launches with coalition partners and up to 100 volunteers showing up to canvass, pick up materials, or help with trainings
asked_others_desc: Yes anytime a canvasser showed noticeable excitement, a certain level of engagement with the campaign, and showed up more than once or twice I always asked them to be a field lead. initially most people responded with surprise and uncertainty about being able to do it but a lot of them did end up becoming leads! It usually took 2-3 conversations with the same person

final_thoughts: I was sort of hoping to come out of this campaign with a better sense of what made it successful. I remember many politically-aligned people rolling their eyes a bit at the "let's knock one million doors" focus, but didn't really see a clear alternative of what volunteers could do to help the campaign succeed. While I had one or two conversations at doors that felt like meaningful persuasion, canvassing felt more like an excercise in polling particular neighborhoods at different times and sometimes watching them change from "never heard of the guy" to "oh I'm totally supporting him". I fear that the means of replicating a campaign like this to obtain a successful result feels a bit mystifying to me--in many ways, it felt like it simply happened organically.

canvass_story:
Early days in my first canvass as a lead, I talked to a young Orthodox guy about the campaign, he said he'd recently registered to vote for the November election and he was actually incredibly receptive about the campaign goals. I reminded him to vote in the primary. And then he asked if he could vote for Zohran as a registered Republican.

To me it was such a powerful moment of how broad and inclusive the message was and how it could actually bring way more people into the fold that wouldn't consider themselves leftists or socialists at all.

More an overarching experience, but getting to see my weekly Bay Ridge canvasses grow and the crew sustain itself over time, and growing as a lead along with them. 

Also primary day, which was a wild day I'll never forget (so hot out, ugh)
was_asked_more_desc: First was asked to become a field lead and attend field lead training (this was about March, as the campaign was shifting from petitioning to persuasion), and then ahead of the general (about August) was asked to become a neighborhood coordinator (scheduling and helping onboard leads in Bay Ridge and Prospect Lefferts Gardens).

final_thoughts: I have been involved in political campaigns (candidates and issues alike) and organizing for about 20 years, and the Zohran campaign was easily the most impactful experience I've had on a campaign. From helping build cultures and systems from the ground up, to empowering others and being empowered, to the ease of use when it came to campaign systems and tech, to a message I could get behind, they all aligned on Zohran's campaign. It was truly an experience I'll never forget and hopefully through Our Time, DSA, JFREJ, WFP, and other campaigns and groups I'll continue to grow my skills and maintain my communities.

canvass_story: Early in the campaign, Zohran's canvassing strategy was to consolidate voters in areas where he was strongest. I wanted to spread the good news in more neglected areas and went out to East New York, Canarsie, Cypress Hills. Talking to young black men there who naturally struggled with cynicism. canvass_story: My first canvassing experience was with a 90+ year old black woman in Brooklyn. This was in January 2025, so no one knew who Zohran was (especially in Brooklyn) and we discussed Eric Adams and then Zohran and his policies. She was extremely disappointed in Eric Adams and was not hopeful for the future of the city for upcoming generations. She was bed ridden due to her age and had a caretaker full time. She also lived in a rent stabalized apartment that she'd lived in for decades. After we discussed Zohran, she thanked us for coming by and commented on the importance of what we were doing. She had been alive during a time when she did not have the legal right to vote, so she now makes a point to vote in every single election, no matter what. But she still finds it hard to learn about every candidate and said when she was watching news coverage, there was nothing about Zohran - but there was content about many other candidates (including Cuomo who I believe was not even officially in the race at this point). She was very happy we came by and told us to keep it up. For it being my first time ever canvassing, it really cemented how meaningful it can be to someone. I felt incredibly lucky to have met her and be able to talk to her - someone I otherwise would probably never have interacted with. It made me so deeply appreciative of the experience and made me feel like I was making an actual difference even so early on in the campaign.
was_asked_more_desc:
After canvassing once or twice, I was added to a WhatsApp group where they said they needed more office help. I had just finished a contract job and suddenly had a lot more time on my hands, so I started going to the office every day. My first day, everyone introduced themselves and I met Tascha. We all did work and after a few days of coming back - I think at the end of my first week - Tascha asked me if I would keep coming back until the end of the campaign. I said yes and she asked me if I would hold a key for the office. So during the entire primary, I had a keycard and a physical key to our primary office location. At the time, it felt quite important and later on the importance was recemented when Zohran started needing full time security. I was trusted enough to keep this key and be able to go whenever I wanted.
After she gave me the key, I kept coming back and the campaign staff would ask me to do more and I would keep saying yes.
asked_others_desc: I personally recruited a few volunteers to become field leads. I was really proud to be a mentor figure to some younger volunteers, mostly who were just out of high school or in the first few years of college. Specifically Lola. After she canvassed on the UES a lot, we became good friends and she spoke a lot about how much she was enjoying canvassing. As a native New Yorker who grew up in the projects, she didn't always love the city, but was having a great time talking to neighbors. I started asking her to help me pass out lit and answer questions from new canvassers. I would always pair her up with someone new to canvassing so she could show them the ropes. After about 6 weeks, I asked her to become a field lead. She got trained up and lead under me on the UES for a little while, but then started also leading elsewhere. She seemed nervous at first to become a lead, doubting her abilities, but I think being able to lead with me in a place she was comfortable gave her more confidence. Now she's a lead for another campaign!

I thought that when Democrats talked about saving lives in Ukraine they meant it, and I didn't understand why the party supported death and destruction in Palestine. canvass_story:
I was a field lead on the Upper West Side where volunteers encountered both significant support and opposition to the campaign. Some of the most negative encounters stick with me as experiences that opened my eyes to Islamophobia, but what sticks with me much more was the resilience of volunteers. 

On several canvassing shifts I helped volunteers process negative encounters that anyone might understandably stop canvassing after. I emphasized that they did not need to push themselves past their comfort zone and had done more than enough if they felt like calling it quits. But every single time I talked to a volunteer who had an experience like this they ended up going back out and finishing their list. That resilience speaks to how much we believed in the work, in DSA, and in Zohran.
was_asked_more_desc: I was asked to become a field captain for the GOTV during the primary campaign. This meant leading a team of field leads, managing supplies, liaising with our launch site host, and overseeing all of our Upper West Side events for the last four days of the campaign. I can't remember how the ask was made, likely by Ravi, but it was an easy yes.
asked_others_desc:
I helped connect some of our great volunteers to field lead trainings. I asked field leads to take on responsibility for running events during GOTV.

I put a lot of emphasis on recruiting to NYC-DSA during the campaign. When I started we were polling at 1% and I thought this was one of the best lasting outcomes I could contribute to as a volunteer. I made a lot of individual asks for people to attend DSA 101s and group asks to join DSA. The response was generally positive and I believe some people did join the organization thanks to these asks.

final_thoughts:
The work I did on the Zohran campaign was the best work I had ever done in my life. The campaign turned me into an organizer and transformed me as a person. My life is unrecognizable before and after it. 

I knew from the beginning that I wanted to be part of something larger than one election, so I joined NYC-DSA at the same time that I started volunteering. I was stunned to learn at my DSA 101 in December 2024 that the organization was 100% volunteer run. (Since then two staff members have been hired.) I'm now one of those volunteers, dedicating all of the free time I can find, about 20 hours a week, to the organization. I hold an elected position in a working group and in a caucus. 

I wish everyday I could put more of my time and energy into organizing. I'm now in law school seeking a degree that can provide me some stability in a future job. I hope to transition to a role in politics someday -- something I never would have contemplated before this campaign. I've had opportunities to interview for jobs in the DSA orbit since the campaign ended but a crucial issue is that they can rarely guarantee more than a handful of months of employment. 

In addition to affecting my career and life trajectory, the Zohran campaign helped me develop an enormous number of skills. I improved at public speaking, managing large groups, delegating tasks, having difficult conversations, and making organizing asks. 
 
A final note is that I think two macro events had some influence on the availability of "super volunteers" (those of us who were basically treating this like a job by April/May/June 2025) -- the Affordable Care Act and Trump's cuts to grant funding and programs like PeaceCorps and AmeriCorps. Many of us who could dedicate the most time to volunteering were unemployed. I quit my job about a month earlier than I had planned so that I could dedicate more time to it, and I was able to do that because I could get health insurance through the ACA. I talked to a number of volunteers who had more time available because programs they were in through the federal government had funding cut or were completely ended. I think the availability of health insurance and the sudden drop off of opportunities for recent college graduates who wanted to "do good" contributed to the availability of volunteers.

tipping_moment: I was asked to sign up for the very first field lead training by Ravi (who’d I’d met through the Claire campaign and his AOC involvement) in January 2025 I believe? I knew I was going to be involved after hearing Z speak at the nycDSA convention at church of the village (I was there to sing with Sing in Solidarity), but had figured it’d just be as a regular volunteer, until I was asked.
asked_by: Ravi (as noted above) - anyone who knows him knows that he’s a capital-N Nice Guy (non derogatory). He doesn’t ask for favors often, so when he does ask for something, you listen. I was honestly flattered to even be considered field lead material (I was a field lead for Claire’s Assembly race, but I don’t think I’m a charismatic person/great public speaker in any way, it was more “you reliably show up” that led them to ask me to do it - most “small” local campaigns are still desperate for field leads so they’ll take almost anyone)

canvass_shifts: Every week from Feb to Primary day, then a few months more before the general
canvass_neighborhoods: 99% of the time in my own (Woodside/Sunnyside), but also did a couple in Jackson Heights, once in Jamaica, and was at the big field launch in Brooklyn at the library with like 500+ people. Also was at the inaugural Astoria canvass
field_lead_training: Yes, I attended a group training.
canvass_easy: Using the canvassing app or materials
canvass_story: Honestly it felt like we were barometers of media coverage’s effects more than the ones influencing voters - I just remember over time seeing more people know who he was (vs the beginning of canvassing when nobody knew), and thinking “thank you Zohrans comms team for doing the work for us”. Persuasion feels like an illusion sometimes - it’s more like our existence alone was proof to people he had a chance more so than what we said (it was mainly older voters who were the most openly saying “he can’t do any of those things he promises” vs younger voters who wanted to believe he could)
was_asked_more_desc: Mainly just field coordinators asking us to fill in as leads for canvasses in other neighborhoods if the regular field lead was gone/sick. I usually said yes, as long as it was in Queens, because it still felt like every shift mattered (& people weren’t gone/sick that often)
post_collab: Volunteered together on another campaign

final_thoughts: The hardest part about being a field lead was dealing with a reoccurring canvasser who was on the spectrum/had obvious learning disabilities, who made various remarks that made (especially) women uncomfortable, but the campaign refused to ban him from canvassing (I think each borough had canvassers like this). As a field lead it was stressful enough to get everyone sorted - to have to babysit another person on top of it was a lot sometimes. But I never wanted to (& still never want to) get paid for doing any volunteering - it would corrupt the sanctity of the act. Seeing paid petitioners still makes me think less of a candidate - if you don’t have the grassroots support, then why are you running?

tipping_moment: I’d been seeing videos of him posted by friends and I thought the videos were both funny and deeply principled, but what really tipped me over into volunteering was that moment in the first debate when he was pressed about Israel. He could have caved, wavered, capitulated, equivocated, but he did none of those things. “I believe in a state with equal rights.” So principled, so succinctly passionate, so well-articulated, true… He communicated antizionist ideas into a distinctly American liberal vernacular in a way no one has done on the national stage in my lifetime. I was shook. This guy in that moment literally broadened the realm of possibility of leftist politics, of — dare I say — what it even means to be Jewish in America (I’m Jewish). I thought to myself “oh my fucking god. I have to put every OUNCE of my BEING into getting this guy elected. He’s the real fucking deal.” Then I canvassed for him 25+ times, field led for him in BedStuy, Harlem, Rego Park, LES… boom. Done deal.

canvass_story: Canvassing for Zohran changed my life. When I think about it, I literally get teary. Talking to every day New Yorkers about the material issues that affect, talking to them across huge ethnic material political and cultural differences, finding solidarity in collective struggle… this is the stuff that is out of our phones, that is relational, connective, true… Canvassing for Zohran was one of the more human, connective things I’ve ever experienced in my life. And I never felt dirty or bad about it because I 100% believed in this candidate. I would never be able to say that about any machine politician that won’t explicitly call themselves a socialist, focus on class politics over culture politics, and stand for Palestinian liberation without equivocation. Any politician that doesn’t have this deeply principled moral center will not bring out this same sense of fervent rabid commitment to the cause of getting them elected. I will definitely canvass for AOC, for example, if she runs for president, but despite her many good positions, she has equivocated far too much on vital leftist priorities and not displayed a true commitment to leftist principles (she’s voted for and defended support to the Iron Dome, she sided w the Biden admin against the train workers’ strike, she supported the IHRA definition of antisemitism — she has rebuttals to all these claims but they are substantively weak), to really instill in me a similar visceral emotional devotion to her victory.

final_thoughts:
I’ve lived in New York City since I was 18, and I’ve been in the city for 13 years. I’ve always loved New York and have always planned on spending the rest of my life here, but being a part of this campaign deepened my love for this city in a way that I can’t even fully put into words because it’s so powerful an emotion. Loving the chaos, the diversity, the love of your fellow neighbor, the solidarity in struggle, the history, the sweaty grittiness of it all, it made me love this city in a way I didn’t even think was possible. I read the power broker after Zohran won lol because I just became so urbanism-pilled. It awoke in me this fierce level of NYC patriotism that I didn’t even think was possible. And it also jolted me out of just being an armchair socialist / black-pilled Chapo-listener to an active DSA political organizer now. I will be forever grateful to that man and his campaign team for that. Zohran changed my life.

SOCIALISM FORVER!!!!!!! NYC FOREVER!!!!!!!!!! SOLIDARITY FOREVER!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

canvass_story: I had a conversation with a middle- aged Black woman living in rent-stabilized housing who had not previously heard of Zohran. She was initially going to vote for Cuomo because of name recognition, but I convinced her otherwise. When she heard about Zohran’s rent freeze she seemed a little skeptical it was possible, but when she heard about Cuomo’s contributions from and ties to the real estate industry and landlord lobby, she saw that as corrupt and against the interests of tenants. By using my experience facing off against Cuomo in the 2019 HSTPA fight as a staffer for Julia Salazar, I was able to convince her to vote for Zohran as a candidate who will support tenants, not landlords.

canvass_story: I don't think I'll forget my first ever Zohran canvass. December 14, 2024 in Astoria. We were exclusively canvassing rent stabilized buildings. We had a conversation with a woman who had just moved into her apartment. She had never heard pf Zohran before. Once she heard Zohran would freeze her rent, she was sold. We listened to what she cared about and how much she hated Eric Adams. She even donated right there! Never had I gotten a donation at the door before or after that canvass ever again. That conversation made me realize this campaign would be very different from anything we had ever done before.
was_asked_more_desc:
Katie Riley and Grace Han asked me to help out with fundraisers and campaign events. I said yes because it felt like an honor to be to be asked by the deputy campaign manager.
Ravi Sahai asked me to help with field lead training and I was very excited because I had only ever trained people how to canvass before, not how to train other people how to canvass. It was very exciting and became my favorite part of the campaign.
Andrew Epstein asked me to film a video in Polish for a campaign video, and I of course agreed and it felt very nice to have my first language acknowledge and be used in promotional material.
asked_others_desc: I asked one of my most dedicated, regular canvassers, Shashank RK to become a field lead. It took him a couple times to be convinced to do it, but he eventually did and started leading his own Woodside Thursday shifts.

canvass_story: In the weeks leading up to the primary I was canvassing all over Brooklyn and older voters and people of color were excited about Zohran and even giving shout outs in the streets where earlier in the campaign it seemed only whiter and younger voters were familiar with him. That was when I knew he had broken out of his social media millienial containment into the wider public and felt confident that he was on track to win.
was_asked_more_desc: Someone in the Citywide Leadership Committee asked me to be a Field Lead for the General Election. If they would have asked me to do it in my home neighborhood of Bushwick where Zohran got around 90% of the vote, I might have said "Bushwick doesn't need me." But they called on me to be a field lead in Brownsville where Zohran had lost the primary. This was part of the "East Brooklyn" zone, a zone that did not receive regular volunteer canvasses during the Primary. I had been a high school teacher in Brownsville before so felt up the challenge and was the princiapl and only consistent field lead there for the duration of the general election. Brownsville saw a roughtly 30 point swing towards Zohran between the Primary and General election which I felt great pride and a little bit of ownership about.
asked_others_desc: I asked a friend to be a field lead but they were too afraid to commit their time so regularly.

canvass_story: During a Sunset Park shift, I spoke to a woman who ran a daycare primarily for the children of immigrants. She told me about how the families all had go bags (the kids didn't know what they were), and how the children would often tell her they loved her before leaving because they didn't know if they would be back the next day. It was one of the few times I was truly speechless canvassing — just an appalling thing to hear how afraid so many of our neighbors were, and it made me even more committed to building a safer and more just New York.

dsa_affiliation: I was a member of DSA prior to the campaign.
heard_about_other: I was close to NYC-DSA leadership in summer 2024 and was aware of initial conversations Zohran was having within the organization to explore a run. I was then elected to NYC-DSA leadership in October 2024 and was directly consulted on his plans to seek NYC-DSA endorsement and launch a mayoral campaign.
tipping_moment: In December 2024 there was an effort from the nascent campaign field operation to "soft launch" canvassing in several different geographic areas as a test case for what turnout and enthusiasm would look like. As someone serving in NYC-DSA leadership with prior canvassing experience, but limited capacity at the time to become a full time field volunteer, I was asked to helped launch the first field event in my neighborhood--mostly in a senior support role for brand new, first-time volunteers who would ultimately be the full-time field leads.  Despite my hesitance to take on a new volunteer role at the time, there was shockingly large organic turnout at this first event that convinced me I had to clear time in my schedule to volunteer regularly going forward.
asked_by: I think it was either Tascha Van Auken or Daniel Goulden who asked me to volunteer for this first event. I had organizing relationships with both of them through DSA. I recall the ask being more of a favor ("Hey, I know you are busy and didn't express interest in volunteering, but we could use help from experienced field organizers at this first event")

canvass_story: Still early in the primary campaign (perhaps March or April?) I was field leading, and we always asked attendees to introduce themselves and share if it was their first time canvassing.  I pretty quickly clocked a volunteer who seemed very nervous to be there and decided I would walk with her to model the process. As we walked and talked, she shared some of her anxieties about talking to strangers in general and about politics specifically. She also shared that the social media and video component of the campaign, and the specific emphasis on showing regular people volunteering, gave her confidence to step out of her comfort zone and come to this event. As we canvassed for close to 3 hours, I watched this person turn from very apologetic and apprehensive, to quite confident in herself and willing to do entire floors of doors alone.  This person ended up coming back frequently for canvasses. I had abstractly believed there could be a transformative component to this particular strategy and process, but I hadn't previously seen it happen so acutely in the course of one day.
was_asked_more_desc: I was asked by Elle Bisgaard-Church and Cea Weaver to contribute to housing platform policy development in addition to the field work I had been doing. I said yes.
asked_others_desc: At multiple points I had asked volunteers to consider field lead training, and asked tentative or part-time field leads to take on more permanent shifts. I think in all cases people responded positively and did take the next step.

final_thoughts: The campaign confirmed many hopes and theories I previously held about electoral work and movement organizing at a scale I never imagined possible.  I have a level of confidence about movement building--not in the abstract, but in specific, strategic ways--that I did not have before this campaign. But mostly, as someone who was already in leadership and organizing on the left prior to the campaign, it was extremely affirming and powerful to see so many other people personally transformed by the experience of participating with agency and ownership in a shared political project for the first time.
